title: Primitive values and typeof

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Primitive values and typeof; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ECMAScript Language Specification; living specification accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Primitive values and typeof
sources.claim: JavaScript primitives are immutable values including undefined, null, Boolean, number, bigint, string, and symbol.

sources.claim: JavaScript primitives are immutable values including undefined, null, Boolean, number, bigint, string, and symbol.

canonicalExplanation.summary: JavaScript primitives are immutable values including undefined, null, Boolean, number, bigint, string, and symbol.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Changing one character creates a new string value rather than mutating the existing primitive. Your goal is to classify primitive and object values and explain null and undefined use cases. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Scripts and console; pf state expressions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: JavaScript primitives are immutable values including undefined, null, Boolean, number, bigint, string, and symbol. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Wrapper objects differ from primitives, number has NaN and infinities, and bigint does not mix implicitly with number arithmetic. This draft therefore rejects: Strings are mutable arrays because bracket access returns characters.

includes: Use undefined, null, Boolean, number, bigint, string and symbol with accurate type expectations.
Classify primitive and object values and explain null and undefined use cases.
excludes: Wrapper objects differ from primitives, number has NaN and infinities, and bigint does not mix implicitly with number arithmetic.

outcomes: Classify primitive and object values and explain null and undefined use cases.

examples.title: Primitive values and typeof: concrete case
examples.situation: Changing one character creates a new string value rather than mutating the existing primitive.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Changing one character creates a new string value rather than mutating the existing primitive.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: String primitives are immutable; operations return new values rather than changing characters in place.
examples.result: The result follows because String primitives are immutable; operations return new values rather than changing characters in place.

examples.title: Primitive values and typeof: boundary case
examples.situation: Wrapper objects differ from primitives, number has NaN and infinities, and bigint does not mix implicitly with number arithmetic.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Wrapper objects differ from primitives, number has NaN and infinities, and bigint does not mix implicitly with number arithmetic.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Strings are mutable arrays because bracket access returns characters.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: String primitives are immutable; operations return new values rather than changing characters in place.
